patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 0/2] mm: don't apply task mempolicy to unmovable kernel allocations

version_history.subject: [PATCH 0/2] mm: don't apply task mempolicy to unmovable kernel allocations

series_items.subject: [PATCH 1/2] mm/filemap: place page-cache folios via an explicit mempolicy
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the Linux kernel's filemap module to explicitly specify a memory policy for page-cache allocations, rather than relying on the implicit behavior of following the first-touch task's memory policy. This change codifies the existing behavior and ensures that pagecache placement remains stable across future changes. The modification involves adding an explicit call to `folio_alloc_mpol_noprof` in the `filemap_alloc_folio_noprof` function, which takes into account the current task's memory policy for allocation decisions.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 2/2] mm/mempolicy: skip task mempolicy for unmovable unaccounted kernel allocations
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the Linux kernel's memory policy to skip following a task's memory policy for certain types of unmovable and unaccounted kernel allocations. These allocations, such as those made by interrupt handlers or explicit placement calls, should not be influenced by a task's memory policy. The patch introduces a new helper function, alloc_task_policy(), to identify these allocation types and prevent them from following the task's policy. This change is intended to prevent performance regressions on tiered memory systems where incidental allocations can land on slower memory tiers.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series prevents a task's NUMA memory policy from influencing incidental kernel allocations that have no direct relationship to the task. Currently, these allocations follow the task's policy, which can lead to poor placement on tiered-memory systems and cause regressions. The fix is to only apply the task policy to movable allocations explicitly tied to the task via __GFP_ACCOUNT, while preferring node-local placement for unmovable kernel allocations. This change ensures that kernel memory is placed in a way that minimizes performance regressions and maintains existing behavior for page cache placement.

review_comments.author: Huang, Ying
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Huang, Ying expressed theoretical agreement but requested empirical evidence of the patch's benefits.

Reviewer noted that the patch does not handle the case where a task's mempolicy is set to MPOL_F_LOCAL, which can lead to incorrect placement of unmovable kernel allocations.
sentiment_signals: patch needs additional handling for MPOL_F_LOCAL policy
review_comments.raw_body:
Personally, I think this should be the right thing to do theoretically.
However, you may need to find some practical issues that this resolves.

---

---
Best Regards,
Huang, Ying

review_comments.author: Gregory Price (author)
review_comments.summary: The author acknowledges that there is at least one scenario where the patch improves performance, specifically when BIOS-configured CXL memory is brought up in ZONE_NORMAL and a task interleave policy causes unaccounted kernel allocations to land on the remote node. The author agrees that this is an improvement but notes it's not a complete fix and plans to send a v2 with additional fixes.
review_comments.raw_body:
I don't entirely disagree, but there is at least one scenario where this
is an obvious improvement:

  BIOS-configured CXL memory brought up in ZONE_NORMAL. 

An task interleave policy on such a system will end up with unaccounted
kernel allocations landing on the remote node, which is just not
preferable at all (and uncorrectable).

It's not a complete fix (fallbacks can still occur under pressure), but
it's one piece of the puzzle.


Forward looking:  This patch makes private-node's with ZONE_NORMAL
reliably hot-un-pluggable.  But that improvement is obviously predicated
on work that isn't upstream (yet :] ).

I need to send a v2 of this with SLAB_ACCOUNT fixed up, and some numbers
to justify dropping the pagecache fix.  But I will probably sandbag this
a bit until i finally send out v5 of private nodes.

~Gregory

review_comments.author: Huang, Ying
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer noted that kernel-allocated memory in ZONE_NORMAL and ZONE_MOVABLE may not follow the task's mempolicy, as these zones are not necessarily tied to the task

Reviewer noted that the patch does not handle the case where a task's mempolicy is set to MPOL_F_LOCAL, which would cause unmovable kernel allocations to still follow the task policy.
sentiment_signals: patch needs additional handling for MPOL_F_LOCAL
review_comments.raw_body:
Per my understanding, kernel configured CXL memory can be in ZONE_NORMAL
and BIOS-configured one can be in ZONE_MOVABLE.  Right?

---

---
Best Regards,
Huang, Ying

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v1 0/2] fuse: fix missing barriers in io-uring init

version_history.subject: [PATCH v1 0/2] fuse: fix missing barriers in io-uring init

series_items.subject: [PATCH v1 1/2] fuse: fix missing barrier when checking io-uring readiness
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a potential issue in the FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) kernel module where a CPU might observe that io-uring initialization is complete before it actually is, leading to skipped checks and incorrect behavior. The fix involves adding a read barrier to pair with an existing write barrier, ensuring that the two loads are properly ordered. This patch addresses a problem introduced by a previous commit (3393ff964e0f) and is intended to prevent issues related to io-uring initialization.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v1 2/2] fuse: publish io-uring queues with release semantics
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a concurrency issue in the io-uring implementation for FUSE file systems by using smp_store_release() to publish the queue pointer and smp_load_acquire() to read it on the lockless fast path. This ensures that the reader sees a fully initialized queue if it sees a non-null queue, preventing operations on uninitialized memory. The patch addresses a problem where concurrent readers could see a non-null queue but uninitialized fields, leading to potential crashes or data corruption.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: The patch series addresses two issues in the fuse driver related to io-uring initialization, specifically missing barriers that can lead to data corruption or incorrect behavior. The patches introduce additional barriers to ensure correct synchronization and fix potential bugs.

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong

review_comments.summary: Acknowledged that some of the additional barriers are not necessary, but expressed concern about Sashiko's analysis and suggested a way to help Sashiko understand the correct behavior.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Mon, Jul 13, 2026 at 3:11 PM Joanne Koong <joannelkoong@gmail.com> wrote:
>
> On Mon, Jul 13, 2026 at 10:54 AM Joanne Koong <joannelkoong@gmail.com> wrote:
> >
> > These are two pre-existing issues Sashiko reported [1] on the fuse zerocopy
> > series.
> >
> > [1] https://sashiko.dev/#/patchset/20260630211436.2062816-1-joannelkoong%40gmail.com
>

> Will send v2 to add these places as well.

These additional ones aren't real bugs. They're not reachable on a
well-behaved server and on a malicious/buggy server, the failure is
benign / self-correcting.

I think we need some sort of way to help Sashiko understand so it
doesn't keep flagging this on every future patch anyone submits. I
think there's a few other places where some of Sashiko's
assumptions/analysis are wrong (eg uring-cmd sqe stability semantics
[1] in the zero-copy series). I'll look into this unless anyone
already knows how to do this.

Thanks,
Joanne

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v5 0/6] fuse: add io-uring buffer pools and zero-copy

version_history.subject: [PATCH v5 0/6] fuse: add io-uring buffer pools and zero-copy

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 1/6] fuse: decouple fuse_ring creation from ent registration
series_items.patch_summary: This patch decouples the creation of fuse_ring from entry registration in FUSE connections. The ring is now created during initialization after a server has negotiated and set FUSE_OVER_IO_URING, rather than being lazily created on the first FUSE_IO_URING_CMD_REGISTER command. This change eliminates concurrent creation races and simplifies the code by removing open-coded handling in fuse_uring_create().

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 2/6] fuse: add FUSE_IO_URING_CMD_ADD_QUEUE
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new FUSE_IO_URING_CMD_ADD_QUEUE command to the Linux kernel's fuse module, allowing servers to create queues explicitly and decoupling queue setup from entry registration. This change is necessary for the introduction of buffer pools, which require existing queues to be created first. The patch modifies the fuse_ring_queue creation logic to handle explicit queue creation and adds a new function fuse_uring_add_queue to handle this command.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 3/6] fuse: add io-uring buffer pools
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds support for buffer pools to the FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) io-uring implementation, allowing the server to pass a contiguous chunk of memory to the kernel for servicing requests. This decouples the buffer allocation from individual entries and enables the kernel to optimize memory usage by reusing buffers across multiple requests. The patch introduces a new command, FUSE_IO_URING_CMD_ADD_BUFPOOL, for registering a buffer pool per queue, and modifies the io-uring implementation to use a bitmap to track free buffers and report their byte offset to the server.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 4/6] fuse: support registered buffer pools in io-uring
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds support for registered buffer pools in io-uring, allowing servers to use a shared buffer pool that is also registered through io-uring. This eliminates the overhead of pinning and unpining user pages per I/O request, improving performance on write-intensive workloads. To use this feature, servers must set the FUSE_URING_REGISTERED_BUFPOOL flag when adding a bufpool and include the index of the registered buffer pool in every submitted sqe. The patch includes benchmark results showing improved throughput for direct writes using registered buffers.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 5/6] fuse: add zero-copy over io-uring
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds zero-copy support to the FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) io-uring implementation, allowing for direct access to client pages or page cache folios without intermediate memory copies. This is achieved by registering relevant pages into a sparse slot in the server's io_uring registered buffer table and using fixed buffer operations. The patch requires CAP_SYS_ADMIN capability and bufpools, and introduces a new flag FOPEN_IO_URING_ZERO_COPY for servers to specify which open files should use zero-copy. The request flow is modified to skip data copying when possible, reducing memory overhead and improving performance.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 6/6] docs: fuse: document io-uring buffer pool and zero-copy uapi
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds documentation for using the FUSE file system over io-uring, specifically covering buffer pools and zero-copy functionality. It introduces new commands and flags to enable these features, including adding a queue, registering a buffer pool, and enabling zero-copy. The documentation is split across three files: fuse-io-uring.rst, index.rst, and uapi/io-uring.rst. This patch aims to provide clear guidance for users on how to utilize the FUSE file system with io-uring's advanced features.

He also suggested including the details inline in fuse-io-uring.rst instead of having a separate file.

Thanks,
Amir.

interacts with it.

Thanks,

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series adds support for io-uring buffer pools and zero-copy to the FUSE file system. The current implementation tightly couples entries and buffers, requiring a dedicated payload buffer for each entry. This patch decouples them by allowing servers to pass in a buffer pool that the kernel can use as needed. Additionally, it introduces zero-copy functionality, which allows servers to directly access client pages or page cache folios without copying data through an intermediary buffer. The benefits of this patch include reduced memory usage requirements and improved throughput, particularly when backing I/O is not the bottleneck.

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed concerns about potential buffer leaks, head-of-line blocking, and resource management in the zero-copy patch. They explained that there is no buffer leak as the buffers are allocated by userspace and released when the ring is torn down. They also stated that the decoupling of ents and buffers under buffer pools does not introduce additional head-of-line blocking and that buffer recycling does not need to check for stalled requests or idle entries. The author agreed to add a barrier pairing in v6 for robustness and dropped the WARN_ON_ONCE warning.
review_comments.raw_body:
Sashiko had some comments [1]. These are the replies to Sashiko's comments:

Patch 1/6:
1) Will add missing smp_rmb() in v6. Fix for pre-existing missing
smp_rmb() is submitted in [2].

2) Pre-existing issue, fix is submitted in [3].

Patch 2/6:
1) Pre-existing issue, fix is submitted in [3].

Patch 3/6:
1) ->uring_cmd() cancellation happens when a task exits or when a ring
is torn down. In both cases, the buffer is not marked as available
again in the bitmap, but this doesn't matter since the task/ring is
torn down. There's no buffer leak anywhere - the buffer was allocated
by userspace.

2) This introduces no additional head-of-line blocking than what
currently exists when there's no ent/buffer available for a request.
If a server wants to preserve memory and allocate fewer buffers than
entries, then requests have to wait until there are available buffers.
With ents and buffers decoupled under buffer pools, this also opens an
optimization where non-payload requests which require no buffer can
still be serviced when entries are free, but that can be a future
patch.
Buffer recycling does not need to check for stalled requests or idle
entries. The buffer is returned back when an in-flight request
completes, and each completion (commit-and-fetch) automatically
assigns the free buffer to any request waiting to be serviced.

3) It is safe (and expected) to read directly from cmd->sqe. uring_cmd
has different SQE-stability semantics than non-cmd io-uring ops.

4) A payload carrying request is added to the tail of the queue and
does not jump ahead of other payload carrying requests. Only
payloadless requests would get serviced first in the case of
bufferpool exhaustion, which is safe since the requests that can be
reordered are independent.

Patch 4/6:
1) It is safe (and expected) to read directly from cmd->sqe. uring_cmd
has different SQE-stability semantics than non-cmd io-uring ops.

2) This race is not reachable on a correct server. For a
buggy/malicious server that fires COMMIT_AND_FETCH concurrently with
ADD_BUFPOOL, this is safe since this returns an error regardless.
There's no regression introduced, but the barrier pairing should be
added anyways for robustness. I'll add this in for v6.

3) No, this is not necessary. See answer to 1)

Patch 5/6:

1) I'll drop the WARN_ON_ONCE. I don't think loopback mount can
trigger the warning, but I think other cases that go through the
kernel_read()/kernel_write() path on a direct-io zero-copy-enabled
fuse file potentially could.

2) This compiles, it is just currently flagged as broken because it
requires the io-uring bvec changes queued up in Jens's tree being
available [4].

3) same answer as above for 2)

4) same answer as the answer for 2) in patch 4.

5) There is no permanent leak - all resources are released when the
ring is torn down. This scenario is only possible if a server
deliberately tries to manufacture it by crossing rings. The worst case
that happens here is that the index in the buffer table of the
original ring remains reserved still and the folios are pinned, until
the ring is torn down. There's no use after free, as io-uring's node
refcount protects against this. Given that zero-copy is already gated
only to privileged servers and all resources are cleaned up when the
ring is torn down, this is not worth fixing, as the server would have
to deliberately self-inflict this.

6) No, this analysis is wrong. io_uring's teardown logic keeps
retrying cancellation until the ctx's references are all dropped (see
io_ring_exit_work()). This is the same in the task exit path as well
(io_uring_cancel_generic()).

Patch 6/6:
1) Will fix the typo for v6.

2) This is fine. It is safe (and expected) to read directly from
cmd->sqe. uring_cmd has different SQE-stability semantics than non-cmd
io-uring ops.

Thanks,
Joanne

[1] https://sashiko.dev/#/patchset/20260630211436.2062816-1-joannelkoong%40gmail.com
[2] https://lore.kernel.org/fuse-devel/20260713175345.2542331-1-joannelkoong@gmail.com/T/#mab904e0a63035e094c18184930284727399174be
[3] https://lore.kernel.org/fuse-devel/20260713175345.2542331-1-joannelkoong@gmail.com/T/#m00c29b80ce418d05a791e330be7a9b9530e4ba99
[4] https://lore.kernel.org/io-uring/20260612184840.4058966-1-joannelkoong@gmail.com/T/#t

patches_acked.patch_summary: Patch to modify node_reclaim() to return the number of pages reclaimed, with minor changes in behavior and removal of unused macros.

review_comments.author: Johannes Weiner
review_comments.summary: Approved the patch with minor changes in behavior, and acknowledged its acceptance with an Acked-by tag.

review_comments.author: Petr Tesarik
review_comments.summary: Identified a missing hunk in the patch that removes unused macros from mm/internal.h, and requested a revised submission.
review_comments.raw_body:
V Mon, 13 Jul 2026 07:04:43 -0400
Johannes Weiner <hannes@cmpxchg.org> napsáno:

> On Fri, Jul 10, 2026 at 05:23:20PM +0200, Petr Tesarik wrote:
> > There is only one caller, get_page_from_freelist(), and it does not make
> > any use of the reason for skipping the reclaim, nor does it make any
> > distinction between a full and partially successful reclaim.
> > 
> > Therefore, node_reclaim() can simply return the number of pages that have
> > been reclaimed, same as __node_reclaim(), and the NODE_RECLAIM_xxx macros
> > can be removed.
> > 
> > There is one small change of behavior when __node_reclaim() was attempted
> > but returned zero. The allocation now skips the zone immediately; before
> > this patch, the zone watermarks were checked first. I believe it was an
> > oversight rather than intention, because the chances that zone watermark is
> > OK after __node_reclaim() did not reclaim any pages are very close to zero.
> > 
> > Originally, I was looking for occurences of NODE_RECLAIM_SOME and
> > NODE_RECLAIM_SUCCESS, but I couldn't find any. That's because they are
> > typecast from the result of a relational operator. This seemed a bit
> > fragile, so I dug a bit deeper and came up with this proposed cleanup.
> > 
> > Signed-off-by: Petr Tesarik <ptesarik@suse.com>  
> 
> Looks good to me, and agree with the minor change in behavior being
> acceptable. With the macros actually removed,

Uh-oh, I missed that hunk when rebasing, and of course, there's no
compiler warning about unused macros. :-(

> Acked-by: Johannes Weiner <hannes@cmpxchg.org>

Thank you. Shall I resubmit once more to include the hunk that removes
the macros from mm/internal.h?

Petr T

patches_acked.subject: Re: [PATCH 2/2] sched/psi: Shut down rtpoll_timer in psi_cgroup_free()
patches_acked.patch_summary: Patch series to shut down rtpoll_timer in psi_cgroup_free() is being reviewed.

review_comments.author: Johannes Weiner
review_comments.summary: Approved the patch series but asked Suren to take a look and provide feedback.

review_comments.author: Suren Baghdasaryan
review_comments.summary: Acknowledged the patch series and expressed willingness to review it, but asked for time to familiarize himself with the implementation.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Mon, Jul 13, 2026 at 3:56 AM Johannes Weiner <hannes@cmpxchg.org> wrote:
>
> On Sun, Jul 12, 2026 at 07:46:19AM -1000, Tejun Heo wrote:
> > psi_schedule_rtpoll_work() is called locklessly from the scheduler hotpath
> > and can race psi_trigger_destroy() taking down the last rtpoll trigger under
> > rtpoll_trigger_lock:
> >
> >   psi_schedule_rtpoll_work()        psi_trigger_destroy()
> >
> >   rcu_read_lock();
> >   task = rcu_dereference(rtpoll_task);
> >                                     rcu_assign_pointer(rtpoll_task, NULL);
> >                                     timer_delete(&rtpoll_timer);
> >   mod_timer(&rtpoll_timer, ...);
> >   rcu_read_unlock();
> >                                     synchronize_rcu();
> >                                     kthread_stop(task_to_destroy);
> >
> > The group can then be freed with the re-armed timer still pending, and
> > poll_timer_fn() runs on freed memory.
> >
> > 461daba06bdc ("psi: eliminate kthread_worker from psi trigger scheduling
> > mechanism") deleted the timer synchronously after the synchronize_rcu(),
> > which prevented this but raced trigger creation instead: the deletion could
> > cancel the timer that a new trigger set armed during the grace period and,
> > as creation also reinitialized the timer at the time, corrupt it.
> > 8f91efd870ea ("psi: Fix race between psi_trigger_create/destroy") moved the
> > initialization into group_init() and the deletion into the locked section,
> > trading the creation races for the window above.
> >
> > Neither placement in the destruction path works. A pending timer firing
> > while the group is alive is harmless though. poll_timer_fn() just wakes the
> > rtpoll waitqueue and doesn't re-arm itself. Bind the timer to the group's
> > lifetime instead and shut it down in psi_cgroup_free(). Nothing can arm it
> > by then. timer_shutdown_sync() because the timer is never armed again.
> >
> > Fixes: 8f91efd870ea ("psi: Fix race between psi_trigger_create/destroy")
> > Cc: stable@vger.kernel.org # v5.10+
> > Reported-by: Sashiko AI <sashiko-bot@kernel.org>
> > Closes: https://lore.kernel.org/all/20260711000434.36C4A1F000E9@smtp.kernel.org/
> > Signed-off-by: Tejun Heo <tj@kernel.org>
>
> Acked-by: Johannes Weiner <hannes@cmpxchg.org>
>
> Both these patches look good to me, but Suren can you please also take
> a look?

Yes, I'm on it. Need some time to remind myself of all the details of
the implementation.

patches_acked.subject: Re: [PATCH 1/2] sched/psi: Create the psimon kthread outside of cgroup_mutex
Re: [PATCH v5] mm: mglru: fix stale batch updates after memcg reparenting

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH] mm/hugetlb: restore failed global reservations to subpool in alloc_hugetlb_folio
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch aims to restore failed global reservations to a subpool in the alloc_hugetlb_folio function, addressing an issue where hugetlb folios are not being properly allocated due to incorrect handling of subpools. The problem arises from competing fixes being proposed for this codepath, and this patch seeks to resolve it by restoring failed reservations to their original subpool. The approach taken involves revisiting the logic surrounding subpool locking and release to ensure correct behavior.

review_comments.author: Ackerley Tng
review_comments.summary: reviewer suggests centralizing the fix for HugeTLB reservations into the subpool, which would allow cleaning up existing codepaths and potentially simplify tracking availability instead of reservations, reducing possible races
review_comments.raw_body:
Thanks for connecting us!

I'd like to make a pitch for centralizing the fix into the subpool [1]
:) I think it will also let us clean up the existing codepaths where
each path does its own open-coding and reaching into the subpool. Also,
if that cleanup is centralized into the subpool, we might be able to
track availability instead of reservations [4], which I think simplifies
HugeTLB reservations and removes some possible races (for keeping
rsv_hpages and resv_huge_pages in sync) completely.

[4] https://lore.kernel.org/all/CAEvNRgGN0HSJ2iLSDD2haSKOxifa-uhkO9Hwossh0+Q_d9fzOw@mail.gmail.com/

I understand the above solution is a deeper change, does anyone have any
thoughts on the approach, or existing tests other than libhugetlbfs and
tools/testing/selftests/mm/ksft_hugetlb.sh (which pass) that I could use
to prove this works?

review_comments.author: Song Hu
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Song Hu noted that the patch's codepath is problematic due to multiple competing fixes being discussed, and specifically questioned why the subpool is unlocked or released in this particular code location.

Reviewer Song Hu noted that unlock_or_release_subpool() should not be used in this error path because it may drop the subpool's last reference, which is not necessary since spool->count is never touched; instead, a plain spin_unlock_irqrestore() is sufficient and clearer.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hi Joshua,

Thanks for the review and for bringing the authors together.

First, apologies for the duplicate submission \u2014 when I sent this I was not
aware of the other proposals on the list ([1][2][3]). I ran into this leak
while looking at the subpool accounting and simply wanted to share a fix;
I did not mean to add noise to an ongoing discussion.

---

Good catch \u2014 it does not belong here. unlock_or_release_subpool() is meant
for paths that may drop the subpool's last reference (e.g.
hugepage_subpool_put_pages(), after which the subpool can become free). In
this error path we only adjust used_hpages and never touch spool->count, so
the subpool cannot be released here; a plain
spin_unlock_irqrestore(&spool->lock, flags) is sufficient and clearer. I
copied it from the hugetlb_reserve_pages() sibling fix (1d3f9bb4c8af), but
it is not the right helper here either; I will switch to
spin_unlock_irqrestore() in v2.

One thing that may be worth keeping in mind for the discussion:
a833a693a490 is already in 6.x -stable, so the leak is present in those
trees as well, and a backportable fix will be needed there regardless of
which direction is chosen for mainline.

For what it is worth, I verified the behavior with a small reproducer (a
size= hugetlbfs mount with nr_hugepages churned 10 -> 2 -> 10: unpatched,
the subpool only maps 2/10 pages after the leak; patched, 10/10), in case
it is useful to whoever ends up shaping the final fix.

Happy to help with whatever direction people want to take.

Thanks,
Song

review_comments.author: Song Hu
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Song Hu noted that the subpool permanently loses capacity without a fix, and provided a reproducer test to demonstrate this issue. He also suggested that a minimal fix may still be wanted on stable branches even if centralization goes in for mainline.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hi Ackerley,

Thanks for the write-up. The centralization reads cleanly, and tracking
availability instead of reservations does look like it would remove a real
class of the rsv_hpages / resv_huge_pages sync races, so I don't have an
objection to that direction for mainline.

On tests \u2014 I put together a small reproducer while debugging this, which
might be useful regardless of which fix lands: a size= hugetlbfs mount (so
the subpool has a max_hpages), with nr_hugepages churned 10 -> 2 -> 10
around a MAP_NORESERVE fault loop. Without a fix the subpool permanently
loses capacity (it maps 2/10 pages even with 10 global pages free); with a
fix it recovers to 10/10.

One thing worth flagging for -stable: a833a693a490 is already in 6.x
-stable, so the leak is live there too, and a larger refactor probably is
not backportable \u2014 so a minimal fix may still be wanted on the stable
branches even if centralization goes in for mainline.

Thanks,
Song

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH] mm: thp: pin the inode across a file folio split

version_history.subject: [PATCH] mm: thp: pin the inode across a file folio split

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch fixes a use-after-free bug in the THP (Transparent Huge Pages) split function by pinning the inode across a file folio split using igrab(). The problem occurs when a concurrent final iput() can evict and RCU-free the inode before __folio_split() is done touching the mapping. The patch introduces a new variable 'inode' to hold the inode reference, which is grabbed with igrab() before the split and dropped with iput() after the last mapping dereference. If the inode is already being evicted, the function returns -EBUSY.

review_comments.author: Zi Yan
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Zi Yan suggested moving the EOF folio drop code to avoid a shmem_uncharge() issue, but agreed that explicit inode pinning is a better solution due to an implicit dependency on inode lifetime.

Gave Acked-by
review_comments.raw_body:
I was thinking maybe we could move the EOF folio drop code in the unlock
loop to avoid the shmem_uncharge() issue you mentioned in the Closes.
But that hides this implicit dependency (I did not know about this inode
lifetime issue until this patch comes out). So I agree that an explicit
inode pinning is a much better solution.

review_comments.author: Andrew Morton
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Andrew Morton noted that iput() is called while holding the folio lock, which could lead to a deadlock due to the interaction between the per-vswap spinlock and the folio lock in reclaim paths.
review_comments.raw_body:
Sashiko is worried about iput() while holding folio_lock():

	https://sashiko.dev/#/patchset/20260713170915.239819-1-kirill@shutemov.name

review_comments.author: Kiryl Shutsemau (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged a deadlock possibility if iput() drops the last reference and @lock_at is still in page cache, but expressed uncertainty about finding an obvious solution.
review_comments.raw_body:
Sashiko is right. If iput() drops the last reference and
@lock_at is still in page cache we would self-deadlock.

I don't see an obvious solution. Will think more tomorrow.

-- 
  Kiryl Shutsemau / Kirill A. Shutemov

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v6 0/3] x86/tdx: Fix port I/O handling bugs

version_history.subject: [PATCH v6 0/3] x86/tdx: Fix port I/O handling bugs

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 1/3] x86/tdx: Fix off-by-one in port I/O handling
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series addresses an off-by-one error in the port I/O handling code for x86/tdx architecture. The issue arises from the use of GENMASK macro, which includes one extra bit in the mask calculation. This results in a wider mask than intended, causing issues with I/O operations. The patch fixes this by adjusting the mask calculation to exclude the extra bit.

review_comments.author: Dave Hansen
review_comments.summary: The patch has been merged into the x86/tdx branch of tip. No comments or feedback provided.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 2/3] x86/insn-eval: Move assign_register() out of KVM as insn_assign_reg()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch moves a helper function, assign_register(), from KVM's instruction emulator to a new header file <asm/insn-eval.h> as insn_assign_reg(). The function writes values into registers following x86 rules for general-purpose register writes. The same logic is needed by the TDX guest #VE handler for port I/O emulation and was previously duplicated in KVM. The patch adds no functional change but makes the code more maintainable and reusable.

review_comments.author: tip-bot2 (Meta)
review_comments.summary: The reviewer noted that the comment in the switch statement for a 32-bit write incorrectly states that it zero-extends into the upper 32 bits, when in fact it should be zero-extending to 64 bits.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 3/3] x86/tdx: Fix zero-extension for 32-bit port I/O
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series addresses a bug in the x86/tdx port I/O handling, specifically for 32-bit operations. The current implementation only masks the lower 32 bits of RAX, preserving the upper 32 bits when emulating a 32-bit port IN instruction. The fix uses insn_assign_reg() to write the result back into RAX with proper partial-register-write semantics.

review_comments.author: tip-bot2 for Kiryl Shutsemau (Meta)
review_comments.summary: The patch was successfully merged into the x86/tdx branch of tip, indicating a positive sentiment from the reviewer.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series fixes bugs in the handling of emulated port I/O operations in the TDX guest #VE handler. The first patch corrects an off-by-one error in the GENMASK() used for I/O size, while the third patch ensures that 32-bit port IN operations zero-extend into RAX as per x86 semantics. The second patch moves a function to a shared header file so it can be reused by both KVM and the #VE handler.

review_comments.author: tip-bot2 (Meta)
review_comments.summary: The reviewer noted that the current implementation of handle_in() only masks the lower 32 bits when emulating a 32-bit port IN instruction, preserving the upper 32 bits of RAX instead of zero-extending to 64 bits as per x86 architecture rules. They suggested using insn_assign_reg() to write the result back into RAX with proper partial-register-write semantics.
review_comments.raw_body:
The following commit has been merged into the x86/tdx branch of tip:

Commit-ID:     941370fc93cc3474e26811f4d3b062903eefe2cf
Gitweb:        https://git.kernel.org/tip/941370fc93cc3474e26811f4d3b062903eefe2cf
Author:        Kiryl Shutsemau (Meta) <kas@kernel.org>
AuthorDate:    Mon, 13 Jul 2026 14:37:53 +01:00
Committer:     Dave Hansen <dave.hansen@linux.intel.com>
CommitterDate: Mon, 13 Jul 2026 14:45:07 -07:00

x86/tdx: Fix zero-extension for 32-bit port I/O

According to x86 architecture rules, 32-bit operations zero-extend the
result to 64 bits. The current implementation of handle_in() only masks
the lower 32 bits, which preserves the upper 32 bits of RAX when a
32-bit port IN instruction is emulated.

Use insn_assign_reg() to write the result back into RAX with proper
partial-register-write semantics: 1- and 2-byte forms leave the upper
bits untouched, the 4-byte form zero-extends to the full register.

Fixes: 03149948832a ("x86/tdx: Port I/O: Add runtime hypercalls")
Reported-by: Borys Tsyrulnikov <tsyrulnikov.borys@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Kiryl Shutsemau (Meta) <kas@kernel.org>
Signed-off-by: Dave Hansen <dave.hansen@linux.intel.com>
Reviewed-by: Binbin Wu <binbin.wu@linux.intel.com>
Link: https://lore.kernel.org/all/CAKw_Dz96rfSQc6Rn+9QBcUFHhmkK+9zu+P=bxowfZwxrATCBRg@mail.gmail.com/
Cc:stable@vger.kernel.org
Link: https://patch.msgid.link/20260713133753.223947-4-kirill@shutemov.name
---
 arch/x86/coco/tdx/tdx.c | 8 +++-----
 1 file changed, 3 insertions(+), 5 deletions(-)

diff --git a/arch/x86/coco/tdx/tdx.c b/arch/x86/coco/tdx/tdx.c
index b8bbd71..f904a63 100644
--- a/arch/x86/coco/tdx/tdx.c
+++ b/arch/x86/coco/tdx/tdx.c
@@ -694,8 +694,8 @@ static bool handle_in(struct pt_regs *regs, int size, int port)
 		.r13 = PORT_READ,
 		.r14 = port,
 	};
-	u64 mask = GENMASK(BITS_PER_BYTE * size - 1, 0);
 	bool success;
+	u64 val;
 
 	/*
 	 * Emulate the I/O read via hypercall. More info about ABI can be found
@@ -703,11 +703,9 @@ static bool handle_in(struct pt_regs *regs, int size, int port)
 	 * "TDG.VP.VMCALL<Instruction.IO>".
 	 */
 	success = !__tdx_hypercall(&args);
+	val = success ? args.r11 : 0;
 
-	/* Update part of the register affected by the emulated instruction */
-	regs->ax &= ~mask;
-	if (success)
-		regs->ax |= args.r11 & mask;
+	insn_assign_reg(&regs->ax, val, size);
 
 	return success;
 }

review_comments.author: tip-bot2 (Meta)
review_comments.summary: The reviewer noted that the patch moves KVM's assign_register() function to <asm/insn-eval.h> and renames it to insn_assign_reg(), but they requested that the patch be rebased on a more recent version of the x86/tdx branch.

review_comments.author: tip-bot2 (Meta)
review_comments.summary: The reviewer noted that the patch incorrectly calculates the mask for port I/O handling, resulting in a one-bit too wide mask for all I/O sizes. They suggested fixing this by subtracting 1 from the calculation of BITS_PER_BYTE * size.
review_comments.raw_body:
The following commit has been merged into the x86/tdx branch of tip:

Commit-ID:     0f63e656b1c679d32ac595de29d10c03efca6a25
Gitweb:        https://git.kernel.org/tip/0f63e656b1c679d32ac595de29d10c03efca6a25
Author:        Kiryl Shutsemau (Meta) <kas@kernel.org>
AuthorDate:    Mon, 13 Jul 2026 14:37:51 +01:00
Committer:     Dave Hansen <dave.hansen@linux.intel.com>
CommitterDate: Mon, 13 Jul 2026 14:45:07 -07:00

x86/tdx: Fix off-by-one in port I/O handling

handle_in() and handle_out() in arch/x86/coco/tdx/tdx.c use:

    u64 mask = GENMASK(BITS_PER_BYTE * size, 0);

GENMASK(h, l) includes bit h. For size=1 (INB), this produces
GENMASK(8, 0) = 0x1FF (9 bits) instead of GENMASK(7, 0) = 0xFF (8
bits). The mask is one bit too wide for all I/O sizes.

Fix the mask calculation.

Fixes: 03149948832a ("x86/tdx: Port I/O: Add runtime hypercalls")
Reported-by: Borys Tsyrulnikov <tsyrulnikov.borys@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Kiryl Shutsemau (Meta) <kas@kernel.org>
Signed-off-by: Dave Hansen <dave.hansen@linux.intel.com>
Reviewed-by: Kai Huang <kai.huang@intel.com>
Reviewed-by: Kuppuswamy Sathyanarayanan <sathyanarayanan.kuppuswamy@linux.intel.com>
Reviewed-by: Binbin Wu <binbin.wu@linux.intel.com>
Reviewed-by: Rick Edgecombe <rick.p.edgecombe@intel.com>
Link: https://lore.kernel.org/all/CAKw_Dz96rfSQc6Rn+9QBcUFHhmkK+9zu+P=bxowfZwxrATCBRg@mail.gmail.com/
Cc:stable@vger.kernel.org
Link: https://patch.msgid.link/20260713133753.223947-2-kirill@shutemov.name
---
 arch/x86/coco/tdx/tdx.c | 4 ++--
 1 file changed, 2 insertions(+), 2 deletions(-)

diff --git a/arch/x86/coco/tdx/tdx.c b/arch/x86/coco/tdx/tdx.c
index 29b6f1e..b8bbd71 100644
--- a/arch/x86/coco/tdx/tdx.c
+++ b/arch/x86/coco/tdx/tdx.c
@@ -694,7 +694,7 @@ static bool handle_in(struct pt_regs *regs, int size, int port)
 		.r13 = PORT_READ,
 		.r14 = port,
 	};
-	u64 mask = GENMASK(BITS_PER_BYTE * size, 0);
+	u64 mask = GENMASK(BITS_PER_BYTE * size - 1, 0);
 	bool success;
 
 	/*
@@ -714,7 +714,7 @@ static bool handle_in(struct pt_regs *regs, int size, int port)
 
 static bool handle_out(struct pt_regs *regs, int size, int port)
 {
-	u64 mask = GENMASK(BITS_PER_BYTE * size, 0);
+	u64 mask = GENMASK(BITS_PER_BYTE * size - 1, 0);
 
 	/*
 	 * Emulate the I/O write via hypercall. More info about ABI can be found

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v4] fs/proc/task_mmu: fix PAGEMAP_SCAN written state for PMD holes

version_history.subject: [PATCH] fs/proc/task_mmu: fix PAGEMAP_SCAN written state for PMD holes
[PATCH v2] fs/proc/task_mmu: fix PAGEMAP_SCAN written state for PMD holes
[PATCH v3] fs/proc/task_mmu: fix PAGEMAP_SCAN written state for PMD holes
[PATCH v4] fs/proc/task_mmu: fix PAGEMAP_SCAN written state for PMD holes

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the Linux kernel where PAGEMAP_SCAN incorrectly reports an unpopulated PMD hole as not written, leading to data corruption after live migration or checkpoint/restore operations. The fix involves reporting holes in non-hugetlb uffd-wp VMAs as written and adding a selftest covering the anon-THP PMD-hole case. This patch addresses issues with write tracking and page table management, ensuring that changes made by MADV_DONTNEED are correctly reported to prevent data corruption.

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH mm-hotfixes v2 3/4] mm/ptdump: always stabilise against page table freeing using init_mm
Re: [PATCH mm-hotfixes v2 1/4] mm/vmalloc: acquire init_mm lock on huge vmap to avoid ptdump UAF
Re: [PATCH mm-hotfixes v2 0/4] mm: fix UAF caused by race between ptdump and vmap pgtable freeing
Re: [PATCH mm-hotfixes v2 0/4] mm: fix UAF caused by race between ptdump and vmap pgtable freeing
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch series addresses an out-of-bounds memory access (UAF) caused by a race between the ptdump and vmap pgtable freeing mechanisms in the Linux kernel. The issue arises from inverted locking, where the walker takes the write lock and mutators take read locks, which is subtle and prone to deadlocks. To fix this, the patch proposes deferring the free of page tables using RCU (Read-Copy Update) and making ptdump a lockless walker. This approach would eliminate the need for inverted locking and prevent potential deadlocks, while also improving performance by reducing the load on the init_mm mmap write lock.

review_comments.author: Lorenzo (ARM)
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Lorenzo expressed concerns about backporting a fix that involves RCU-deferring page table freeing, citing potential subtleties and maintenance issues. He also noted that the ptdump walker on x86 needs to take the mmap write lock to exclude munmap operations, making it incompatible with an RCU-free approach.

Reviewer noted that the patch's locking scheme is inverted from normal expectations, which led to an arm64 deadlock and ifdeffery, and suggested using RCU-deferred freeing for ptdump like GUP-fast does

Reviewer noted that the locking scheme in the patch is inverted from what's normally expected, which led to an arm64 deadlock and ifdeffery, and suggested using RCU-deferred free and making ptdump a lockless walker

Reviewer suggested using call_rcu() instead of synchronize_rcu(), citing it as a 'bug hammer', and noted that CPA doesn't mark page tables as kernel

Reviewer noted that the current locking scheme in ptdump is inverted from normal expectations, which led to an arm64 deadlock and ifdeffery, and suggested revisiting the approach by using RCU-deferred free and making ptdump a lockless walker.

The reviewer is concerned that ptdump's walk may stall due to RCU, especially considering vmap can span PUDs and P4Ds, and also worries about the validity of page table entries when read by the walker. They suggest making changes across the kernel for all kernel page table users.
review_comments.raw_body:
Overall I like the idea of eventually moving this to _all_ being
RCU-free-able :)

BUT... I don't like it as a fix for this bug that has to be backported.

I think there's a lot of subtleties to worry about and I don't want to
worry about having to maintain tht as a backport.

But also, we have an unfortunate situation with ptdump where it also walks
userland ranges on x86 (and ranges for efi_mm on both x86 and arm64).

And for userland ranges you have to have the mmap write lock to exclude a
downgraded mmap read lock munmap() operation (which gives rise to the weird
inversion you mention).

So the walker still has to take the write lock in this case.

I spoke a bit about it overall at [0].

We already have this mmap lock convention as a requirement for kernel
ranges, and it was being violated by CPA and vmalloc.

So I'd prefer we keep this as the proximate fix to solve the bug here, and
then revisit this later (alongside moving to RCU page table freeing
_overall_, though one doesn't have to block the other).

I believe Suren is looking at this (and I'm happy to look if Suren is tied
up with other things also!)

---

Yeah :) this is a direct result of the munmap() thing mentioned above
(because ptdump can read non-kernel ranges).

Because you _have_ to take the mmap write lock there, so you _may as well_
exclude with a read lock and then get to do an mmap read trylock as an
added bonus on vmap huge page promotion.

---

Well everybody who does things with shared resources should be aware of
locking requirements.

---

Well I did want to say btw that CPA doesn't actually mark the page tables
as kernel, was going to chase up with something on that when I got a chance
:)

synchronize_rcu() is a very bug hammer, you can just use call_rcu() (and
the ptdesc already has an rcu_head I think).

---

Yeah it all sucks, but you're really making my point that this is better to
revisit and consider carefully rather than doing as a fix.

---

Yes - as above obviously I'd definitely prefer to defer this to a
follow-up.

The main concern for ptdump is properly slicing its walk to avoid
RCU stalls.

Keeping in mind that vmap can (in theory) span PUDs and even P4Ds it
becomes a bit tricky.

But also I worry about whether the entries in the page table will actually
be valid at the point the walker reads them.

For vmap/CPA pretty much yes they are, but if something was to actually
unmap them in future then that might no longer be the case. RCU will only
guarantee that the page tables stick around, not that they contain anything
valid.

In userland a lot of this is solved already by the leaf page table entry's
PTL.

Kernel page tables are a bit of a weird case, because mostly we solve
things through exclusive owners, ptdump being the weirdo outlier.

Anyway TL;DR is that - I think we need to be careful and think this through
and make changes across the board for all kernel page table users and
update the page walkers.

I'm happy to spin up an RFC for this when I get a moment :)

But in general I'd like us to solve the problem as a whole across the
kernel for both userland and kernel page table allocations, ideally!

review_comments.author: Kiryl Shutsemau (author)
review_comments.summary:
The author addressed Lorenzo's concern about the inverted locking scheme in ptdump, explaining that walking user page tables under RCU can make the mmap lock unnecessary and proposing to remove it for all ranges, including kernel ones.

Author agrees to restructure the patch in a follow-up series to address concerns about inverted locking and potential deadlocks, and promises to properly review the current patchset.

Author acknowledged that CPA allocates split tables directly and thus skips IOMMU SVA KVA invalidation, which is an independent issue from the original patch.

Author acknowledged a potential issue with bounding read-side sections during ptdump walks, agreeing it's the part that needs the most care and implying further work is needed.

The author addressed Lorenzo's concern about stale page table entries being accessed by the walker, explaining that since frees are RCU-deferred, the walker can only reach a table via an entry it read within its RCU section and freeing requires unlinking that entry first. The author acknowledged that this approach is sufficient for ptdump but agreed that more care is needed for general cases.
sentiment_signals: agreed it's the part that needs the most care
review_comments.raw_body:
I think the userland side is fixable too.

My first thought was to walk only VMA-backed ranges, which would make
the mmap read lock sufficient: munmap() detaches VMAs under the write
lock before downgrading, and free_pgtables() only frees tables
exclusively covering the detached range.

But that is an unnecessary limitation on MMU_GATHER_RCU_TABLE_FREE
architectures -- which is every architecture with generic ptdump
support. There, user page table freeing is already RCU-deferred; it is
what GUP-fast relies on to walk user page tables blindly with no mmap
lock. khugepaged retracting PTE tables under the pmd lock is likewise
covered by pte_free_defer().

So the downgraded munmap() teardown you mention is already safe to race
with -- for a walker inside an RCU read-side section. The write lock is
only needed today because ptdump walks outside of one, so the deferred
freeing does nothing for it.

That would make the end state: the whole of ptdump -- userland, efi_mm
and kernel ranges -- walks under RCU with no mmap lock taken at all,
and the kernel-side freeing conversion we discussed is the only
missing piece.

---

No argument, let's do it as a follow-up.

I will give the current patchset a proper review.

---

You are right. CPA allocates the split tables with bare
pagetable_alloc(), so ptdesc_test_kernel() is false and collapse frees
them via __pagetable_free() directly. Note this also means they skip
the IOMMU SVA KVA invalidation that ASYNC_KERNEL_PGTABLE_FREE is there
for -- that looks like a bug today, independent of ptdump.

---

Since the walker only reads, I think it is enough to re-descend from
the pgd with fresh READ_ONCE() at each level after dropping RCU --
whatever level got promoted in the meantime, we see either the new leaf
or the old table. But agreed this is the part that needs the most care.

---

For a walker that only reads, I don't think staleness is a problem as
long as frees are RCU-deferred: the walker can only reach a table via
an entry it read within its RCU section, and freeing requires unlinking
that entry first, so the grace period covers any walker that saw the
old pointer. Stale leaf contents are harmless for a dumper. It does
become a problem for anything that dereferences through leaf entries --
agreed that the general case needs more care than ptdump does.

-- 
  Kiryl Shutsemau / Kirill A. Shutemov
